meta.notes: Populated from WayFinder app data and verified travel references. Null fields to be enriched via Tavily web search.

language_and_money.english_proficiency: Low — moderate in Lima tourist areas and Cusco. Very limited in rural areas.
language_and_money.cash_vs_card: Cash preferred in most situations outside Lima. Cards accepted at hotels, upscale restaurants, and malls in major cities.
language_and_money.atm_availability: Widely available in cities. BCP and BBVA Continental most common. Scarce in rural highlands and Amazon. Withdraw in soles to avoid poor exchange rates.
language_and_money.tipping_culture: Not mandatory. 10% service charge often included in restaurants.
language_and_money.tipping_etiquette: Leave 10% if no service charge. Tip trekking porters $5-10/day. Tour guides $10-20/day. Round up taxi fares.

visa_requirements.us_citizen: No visa required for stays up to 183 days

entry_and_border.passport_validity_rule: Must be valid for at least 6 months beyond date of entry
entry_and_border.customs_restrictions: Strict on cultural artifacts — pre-Columbian items cannot be exported. No coca leaves beyond Peru.
entry_and_border.duty_free_allowances: 400 cigarettes or 50 cigars, 3 liters of alcohol, gifts up to $500 value

required_vaccines: Yellow Fever (if entering Amazon regions below 2,300m)
health.malaria_risk: Present in Amazon lowlands below 2,000m — Loreto, Madre de Dios, Ucayali, San Martín
health.malaria_prophylaxis_info: Atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine recommended for Amazon travel. Not needed for Lima, Cusco, Machu Picchu, or highlands.
health.dengue_risk: Moderate to high in lowland areas, especially during rainy season. Present in Lima's outskirts, coastal cities, and Amazon.
health.altitude_sickness_risk: HIGH — Cusco at 3,400m, Puno at 3,800m, Rainbow Mountain at 5,200m, Huaraz at 3,050m. Acclimatize 2-3 days before strenuous activity. Soroche pills and mate de coca help.
health.food_water_safety: Tap water not safe to drink. Bottled water widely available. Street food generally safe at busy stalls. Raw salads risky outside upscale restaurants.
health.air_quality_concerns: Lima can have poor air quality due to traffic and coastal fog (garúa). Highland cities generally good air quality.
health.pharmacy_access: Pharmacies (farmacias/boticas) widely available. InkaFarma and MiFarma chains in every city. Many medications OTC. Limited in remote areas.
health.medical_care_quality: Good private hospitals in Lima (Clínica Ricardo Palma, Clínica Anglo Americana). Limited in provinces. Cusco has basic facilities. Evacuate from remote areas for serious conditions.

risk_indicators: Petty crime high in Lima and Cusco tourist areas
Express kidnapping risk in Lima with unlicensed taxis
Drug trafficking in VRAEM region and border areas
Political protests can block roads, especially in highlands
safety.crime_risk: Moderate — pickpocketing and bag snatching common in Lima (Miraflores, Centro) and Cusco. Callao is high-risk (homicide rate 35/100k). Most tourist areas are safe with precautions.
safety.scam_risk: Moderate — taxi overcharging, fake tour operators in Cusco, counterfeit currency, friendship bracelet scam, fake police.
safety.transport_safety: Use ride-hailing apps instead of street taxis in Lima. Intercity buses safe on major routes — use reputable companies (Cruz del Sur, Oltursa). Night buses generally safe on main highways.
safety.solo_female_travel_notes: Generally safe with precautions. Avoid walking alone at night in cities. Catcalling common. Cusco and Sacred Valley are comfortable for solo female travelers.
safety.lgbtq_notes: No legal recognition of same-sex relationships. Generally tolerant in Lima (Miraflores, Barranco). More conservative in provinces and highlands.
safety.night_safety: Avoid walking alone at night in Lima Centro, Callao, and unfamiliar neighborhoods. Tourist areas in Miraflores and Barranco are reasonably safe. Cusco's Plaza de Armas area is safe until late.
safety.political_stability_notes: Political instability and protests can occur suddenly. Road blockades (bloqueos) common in highlands during protests. Monitor local news. Avoid large gatherings.
common_scams: Express kidnapping — unlicensed taxis, use InDriver/Cabify/Beat instead
Fake police asking to inspect wallet — real police won't do this on the street
Counterfeit soles — learn to identify real bills
Taxi overcharging — agree on price before or use apps
Friendship bracelet scam in tourist areas
Fake tour operators in Cusco — book through hotel or reputable agency
ATM skimming — use bank-attached ATMs, cover keypad
safety.police_reliability: Variable. Tourist police (Policía de Turismo) in Cusco and Lima are generally helpful. File reports at comisaría. Corruption exists.

weather_and_seasonality.best_time_to_visit: May to September (dry season) for highlands and trekking. December to March for coast and surfing. Amazon is hot and wet year-round.
climate_zones: Coastal desert (Costa) — dry, mild Lima 15-28°C, hotter north
Andean highlands (Sierra) — cool, 5-20°C, distinct dry/wet seasons
Amazon basin (Selva) — hot, humid, 25-35°C, heavy rainfall
weather_and_seasonality.dry_season: May to September (Sierra and trekking); year-round on southern coast
weather_and_seasonality.rainy_season: October to April (Sierra); November to April (Amazon, heaviest Jan-Mar)
weather_and_seasonality.surf_season: Year-round — biggest swells April to October (southern coast); December to March (northern coast Máncora area)

average_temp_by_month.Lima: 15-28°C (warmest Jan-Mar, coolest Jun-Aug with garúa fog)

weather_and_seasonality.daylight_hours_by_season: Near equator in the north; 11-13 hours depending on latitude and season

major_airports: Alejandro Velasco Astete International Airport (CUZ) — Cusco
Capitán FAP Carlos Martínez de Pinillos International Airport (TRU) — Trujillo
transport.public_transit_quality: Lima has Metropolitano BRT and Metro Line 1. Cusco has combis (minibuses). Most cities use combis and colectivos — chaotic but functional.
transport.intercity_bus_quality: Excellent long-distance bus network. Cruz del Sur, Oltursa, and ITTSA offer comfortable semi-cama and cama buses. Book in advance for popular routes.
transport.train_network_quality: Limited but scenic — PeruRail and Inca Rail to Machu Picchu (Ollantaytambo-Aguas Calientes). PeruRail Titicaca (Cusco-Puno, luxury). No practical commuter rail.
transport.road_conditions: Main highways (Pan-American, Central Highway) are paved. Mountain roads narrow and winding with sheer drops. Amazon roads often unpaved. Landslides in rainy season.
transport.taxi_safety_notes: NEVER use unlicensed street taxis in Lima — express kidnapping risk. Use InDriver, Cabify, or Beat. In Cusco, use registered taxis or hotel-arranged transport.
ferry_routes: Puno to Bolivian islands on Lake Titicaca
Iquitos river boats to Amazon lodges
transport.border_crossing_notes: Main crossings: Desaguadero/Yunguyo (Bolivia via Titicaca), Aguas Verdes-Huaquillas (Ecuador), La Balsa (Ecuador via Jaén). Bring passport and proof of onward travel.

budget.value_for_money: Excellent — affordable food, accommodation, and transport. Machu Picchu and Inca Trail permits are the main big-ticket items.

connectivity.mobile_data_quality: Good 4G in Lima, Cusco, Arequipa, and major cities. Spotty in remote highlands and Amazon. No coverage on many trekking routes.
connectivity.wifi_quality: Available in most hostels and cafes in tourist areas. Speeds variable (5-30 Mbps). Lima has best connectivity.
connectivity.power_reliability: Generally reliable in cities. Outages possible in rural areas. Bring power bank for trekking and Amazon.

best_areas_to_stay: Miraflores, Lima — safe, walkable, restaurants, ocean views
Barranco, Lima — bohemian, art galleries, nightlife
San Blas, Cusco — artsy neighborhood above Plaza de Armas
Plaza de Armas, Cusco — central, walkable to everything
Ollantaytambo — Sacred Valley base, quieter than Cusco
accommodation.peak_booking_season: June-August (dry season, trekking); Inca Trail permits sell out months in advance
accommodation.accommodation_notes: Book Inca Trail permits 6+ months in advance. Machu Picchu area hotels in Aguas Calientes are overpriced. Sacred Valley is a better base.

local_customs: Greet with a single kiss on the right cheek (women) or handshake (men)
Family is central to social life
Respect for Andean traditions — Pachamama (Mother Earth) is revered
Coca leaf chewing is traditional and legal — do not confuse with cocaine
culture.dress_expectations: Casual but conservative in highlands. Cover shoulders in churches. Layers essential in mountains. Beachwear only at the beach.
culture.religious_sensitivity: Predominantly Catholic with strong Andean syncretism. Religious festivals blend Catholic saints with Inca traditions. Respect processions.
social_norms: Punctuality is relaxed — 15-30 minutes late is culturally normal
Bargaining expected at markets, not in shops or restaurants
Always greet people when entering a small shop or room
Refusing food or drink can be seen as rude
culture.greeting_style: Warm — cheek kiss for women, handshake for men. Use 'Buenos días/tardes/noches'. In Andes, 'Allillanchu' (Quechua hello).
culture.public_behavior_notes: PDA acceptable in moderation. Don't point at people. Blowing your nose loudly at the table is considered rude.
culture.photography_etiquette: Ask permission before photographing indigenous people or traditional ceremonies. Some may request a small tip (S/1-2). No photos at sacred sites without permission.
important_holidays: Inti Raymi (June 24) — Festival of the Sun in Cusco, largest Andean celebration
Señor de los Milagros (October) — massive purple-robed procession in Lima
Semana Santa (Easter) — major celebrations in Ayacucho
Fiestas Patrias (July 28-29) — Independence Day, nationwide celebrations
Carnaval (February) — water fights, parades (Cajamarca is biggest)
legal_restrictions_tourists_often_miss: Must carry passport or copy at all times
Coca leaves cannot be taken out of Peru
Inca Trail requires permit booked months in advance — no walk-ups
Drone permits required in archaeological sites and national parks

signature_dishes: Ceviche — citrus-marinated fresh fish with red onion, chili, and sweet potato. UNESCO Intangible Heritage.
Lomo saltado — stir-fried beef with onions, tomatoes, and fries. Chinese-Peruvian (chifa) fusion.
Ají de gallina — creamy shredded chicken in yellow pepper and walnut sauce
Causa rellena — layered cold mashed potato with avocado, chicken or tuna
Anticuchos — grilled beef heart skewers, popular street food
Rocoto relleno — stuffed spicy pepper, Arequipa specialty
Tiradito — sashimi-style ceviche with Nikkei (Japanese-Peruvian) influence
Pachamanca — underground earth oven with meats and potatoes, Andean tradition
Sopa a la criolla — spiced noodle soup with beef and egg
food.street_food_safety: Generally safe at busy stalls. Lima has world-class street food culture. Exercise caution with ceviche from unrefrigerated vendors.
food.vegetarian_friendliness: Moderate — quinoa, potatoes (3,000 native varieties), and beans available everywhere. Dedicated vegetarian restaurants in Lima and Cusco. Many soups contain meat stock.
food.vegan_friendliness: Challenging outside Lima and Cusco. Growing vegan scene in Miraflores and Barranco.
must_try_drinks: Pisco Sour — national cocktail, egg white, lime, Angostura bitters
Chicha morada — purple corn drink with pineapple, cinnamon, cloves
Inca Kola — bright yellow soda, tastes like bubblegum/cream soda
Chicha de jora — fermented corn beer, Andean tradition
Emoliente — warm herbal drink sold on street corners
Mate de coca — coca leaf tea, helps with altitude sickness
regional_specialties: Andes: pachamanca, cuy (guinea pig), trucha (trout), rocoto relleno
Amazon: juane (rice and chicken in bijao leaf), tacacho (smoked plantain), paiche fish
Arequipa: rocoto relleno, chupe de camarones (shrimp chowder), adobo
food.drinking_water_notes: Never drink tap water. Bottled water available everywhere (S/1-3). Verify ice is from purified water. Mate de coca is safe.
food.alcohol_rules: Legal drinking age 18. No open container laws but public drunkenness can be fined. Pisco is the national spirit — do not call it Chilean.

top_national_parks: Manu National Park — UNESCO biosphere, Amazon biodiversity, jaguars and 1,000+ bird species
Huascarán National Park — Cordillera Blanca, Peru's highest peak (6,768m), world-class trekking
Paracas National Reserve — coastal desert, Ballestas Islands wildlife, sea lions and seabirds
Tambopata National Reserve — Amazon rainforest, macaw clay licks, giant otters
Colca Canyon — twice as deep as Grand Canyon, Andean condor viewing

top_day_hikes.name: Rainbow Mountain (Vinicunca)
top_day_hikes.description: Colorful striped mountain at 5,200m. Iconic Instagram destination. Vicuña and alpaca along the trail. Extremely high altitude.
top_day_hikes.notes: Start from Cusipata (3 hours from Cusco). Acclimatize several days in Cusco first. Horse rentals available for struggling hikers. Arrive early to avoid crowds.

top_day_hikes.name: Colca Canyon Trek
top_day_hikes.description: Trek into one of the world's deepest canyons. Condor viewing at Cruz del Cóndor. Hot springs at Sangalle oasis. Terraced agriculture.
top_day_hikes.notes: Can be done as 1-day condor viewing or 2-3 day trek to oasis. The climb back up is strenuous (1,200m elevation gain). Start early.

top_day_hikes.name: Machu Picchu from Aguas Calientes
top_day_hikes.description: Hike up from Aguas Calientes to Machu Picchu (1.5 hours), explore the citadel, optionally summit Huayna Picchu or Machu Picchu Mountain for panoramic views.
top_day_hikes.notes: Entry tickets must be purchased in advance. Huayna Picchu limited to 400/day — book early. Bus alternative available ($24 round trip).

top_day_hikes.name: Gocta Waterfall Hike
top_day_hikes.description: Hike through cloud forest to one of the world's tallest waterfalls (771m, two-tier). Cock-of-the-rock and torrent duck sightings.
top_day_hikes.notes: Two trails — upper and lower falls. Guide required. Off the beaten path — few tourists. Combine with Kuélap fortress visit.

top_day_hikes.name: Pastoruri Glacier Trek
top_day_hikes.description: Walk to a receding tropical glacier at 5,240m. Puya Raimondi plants (world's largest bromeliad) en route. Easy but high altitude.
top_day_hikes.notes: Part of Huascarán National Park. Glacier has receded significantly. Altitude is the main challenge — acclimatize in Huaraz first.

multi_day_treks.name: Inca Trail to Machu Picchu
multi_day_treks.description: The classic trek to Machu Picchu through cloud forest and Inca ruins. Dead Woman's Pass at 4,215m. Arrives at Sun Gate at sunrise on day 4.
multi_day_treks.notes: Permit required — book 6+ months in advance. Limited to 500 people/day (200 trekkers + 300 porters/guides). Closed in February for maintenance. Licensed operator required.

multi_day_treks.name: Salkantay Trek
multi_day_treks.description: Alternative to Inca Trail passing below the sacred Salkantay peak (6,271m). Diverse ecosystems from glacial to tropical. Arrives at Machu Picchu.
multi_day_treks.notes: No permit required but book with reputable operator. More challenging than Inca Trail. Salkantay Pass at 4,600m. Can be done independently.

multi_day_treks.name: Huayhuash Circuit
multi_day_treks.description: One of the world's greatest mountain treks. Circles the Cordillera Huayhuash with turquoise lakes, 6,000m+ peaks, and extreme isolation. Six passes above 4,500m.
multi_day_treks.notes: Self-supported or guided. Very remote — nearest hospital is Huaraz. Community fees at checkpoints. Altitude and weather are serious challenges.

multi_day_treks.name: Santa Cruz Trek
multi_day_treks.description: Classic Cordillera Blanca trek through Huascarán National Park. Punta Unión pass at 4,750m with views of Alpamayo. Turquoise glacial lakes.
multi_day_treks.notes: Start from Vaquería or Cashapampa (near Huaraz). Can be done independently with donkey support. Park entry fee required.

multi_day_treks.name: Ausangate Circuit
multi_day_treks.description: Trek around the sacred Ausangate peak (6,384m). Rainbow-colored mountains, hot springs, herds of alpaca and vicuña. Includes Rainbow Mountain.
multi_day_treks.notes: Very high altitude — multiple passes above 5,000m. Teahouse/lodge-to-lodge option available. Remote with limited rescue options.

multi_day_treks.name: Choquequirao Trek
multi_day_treks.description: Trek to the 'other Machu Picchu' — a massive Inca ruin site with only a fraction of the visitors. Steep descent into Apurímac canyon and brutal climb back.
multi_day_treks.notes: Round trip from Cachora. No permit needed. Very few tourists. Cable car under construction may change access. Strenuous — 1,500m elevation gain/loss each way.

multi_day_treks.name: Lares Trek
multi_day_treks.description: Cultural trek through Andean villages with hot springs, weaving communities, and mountain passes. Alternative route to Machu Picchu via Sacred Valley.
multi_day_treks.notes: Less crowded than Inca Trail. Ends in Ollantaytambo with train to Aguas Calientes. Good interaction with local communities.

volcanoes: Misti (5,822m) — iconic cone overlooking Arequipa, climbable in 2 days
waterfalls: Gocta Falls (771m) — one of world's tallest, Chachapoyas
Yumbilla Falls (896m) — tallest in Peru, near Chachapoyas
Catarata de Perolniyoc — Sacred Valley, Inca ruins nearby
hot_springs: Aguas Calientes hot springs — at base of Machu Picchu
Lares hot springs — on Lares Trek route
La Calera hot springs — near Chivay, Colca Canyon
lakes: Lake Titicaca — world's highest navigable lake (3,812m), Uros floating islands
Lake 69 — turquoise glacial lake in Cordillera Blanca
Llanganuco Lakes — twin turquoise lakes below Huascarán
Humantay Lake — turquoise glacial lake on Salkantay route
rivers: Amazon River — originates in Peru (Apurímac River source)
Urubamba River — Sacred Valley, whitewater rafting
Colca River — running through Colca Canyon
wildlife: Andean condor (Colca Canyon — best viewing in world)
birdwatching_hotspots: Manu National Park — 1,000+ bird species, one of highest counts anywhere
Tambopata — macaw clay licks at dawn
Abra Patricia — long-whiskered owlet (endemic, critically endangered)
Machu Picchu — Inca wren, Andean cock-of-the-rock
stargazing_spots: Colca Canyon — high altitude, minimal light pollution
scenic_drives: Sacred Valley — Cusco to Ollantaytambo through Inca heartland
Cusco to Puno — high plateau drive past La Raya pass (4,335m)
Nazca to Arequipa — desert and mountain transitions
Huaraz to Yungay — Cordillera Blanca views

surf_spots.name: Máncora
surf_spots.description: Peru's most famous surf and beach resort town. Consistent beach break with mellow waves year-round. Warm water (rare for Peru) due to northerly location above the Humboldt Current. Perfect for beginners and a great social scene.
surf_spots.notes: Warmest water in Peru — no wetsuit needed Dec–Apr. Strong rip currents at some peaks.

surf_spots.name: Puerto Chicama (Puerto Malabrigo)
surf_spots.description: The longest left-hand point break in the world — waves can reach over 2km in length on a big day. Mechanical, peeling left that allows hundreds of meters of riding. A bucket-list wave for any surfer. Cold water, consistent swell April–September.
surf_spots.crowd_level: Medium (pilgrimage spot — surfers come from worldwide)
surf_spots.notes: Bring a 3/2mm wetsuit — water is cold year-round (16–20°C). Best at mid tide. The wave requires paddling back up the point after each ride.

surf_spots.name: Huanchaco
surf_spots.description: Historic fishing village famous for totora reed boats (caballitos de totora) still used by local fishermen — one of the oldest surfing traditions in the world. Consistent left point break with easy access. Great cultural experience alongside surfing.
surf_spots.notes: Cold water — wetsuit required. Watch for fishermen on reed boats in the lineup. UNESCO-recognized fishing tradition.

surf_spots.name: Punta Hermosa
surf_spots.description: Lima's top surf destination, home to multiple world-class breaks. Caballeros is a powerful left point; Señoritas is a more forgiving right. Has hosted major competitions including ISA World Championships. Easy access from Lima (45 min).
surf_spots.notes: Wetsuit essential — cold Humboldt current. Rocky bottom at some breaks. Locals can be territorial at main peaks.

surf_spots.name: Pico Alto
surf_spots.description: Peru's big wave spot. A massive left-hand reef break that can reach 20+ feet during peak swell events. Hosts the annual Pico Alto Big Wave Invitational. Not for the faint-hearted — heavy, consequences reef below.
surf_spots.notes: Only surfed on large swells. Requires jet ski rescue capability for big days. Water temperature 15–18°C — 4/3mm wetsuit needed.

surf_spots.name: Cerro Azul
surf_spots.description: Classic Peruvian left point break. Long, walling lefts that peel consistently during swell season. A favorite escape from Lima crowds with a more relaxed vibe than Punta Hermosa.
surf_spots.notes: Wetsuit needed. Small town with basic amenities. Best on S or SW swell.

surf_spots.name: La Herradura
surf_spots.description: Lima's most accessible surf beach, inside a sheltered crescent bay. Multiple peaks across the beach. Popular with Lima surf schools and beginners due to easier access and wave consistency.
surf_spots.notes: Cold water — wetsuit required. Crowded on weekends. Good for lessons.

surf_spots.name: Lobitos
surf_spots.description: Forgotten gem — a former oil company ghost town that has become a surf haven. Long, consistent lefts with multiple breaks along the point. Growing community of surfers and digital nomads. Warmer water than central Peru.
surf_spots.notes: Remote feel despite improving infrastructure. Surf camps and hostels available. Bring supplies — town is basic.

wildlife_zones.name: Amazon Basin — Madre de Dios
wildlife_zones.notes: Puerto Maldonado is gateway. Tambopata and Manu lodges offer 3-7 day stays. Macaw clay lick at Tambopata is world-famous.

wildlife_zones.name: Paracas National Reserve
best_months: Year-round; June-November for best marine life
wildlife_zones.notes: Ballestas Islands boat tour ('Poor Man's Galápagos'). Combine with Nazca Lines and Huacachina sand dunes. 4 hours from Lima.

wildlife_zones.name: Manu National Park
wildlife_zones.notes: UNESCO World Heritage and Biosphere Reserve. One of most biodiverse places on Earth. Restricted access — must visit with licensed operator. Multi-day trips only.

wildlife_zones.name: Tambopata National Reserve
wildlife_zones.notes: More accessible than Manu. Multiple lodge options. Macaw clay lick (collpa) at dawn is unmissable. 30-minute boat ride from Puerto Maldonado.

wildlife_zones.name: Colca Canyon
best_months: Year-round; May-September for clearest skies and best condor viewing
wildlife_zones.notes: Cruz del Cóndor viewpoint is best place in the world to see condors up close. Early morning (6-9am) for condor thermals. Twice as deep as Grand Canyon.

wildlife_zones.name: Ballestas Islands
wildlife_zones.notes: Boat tour from Paracas ($15-20). Cannot land on islands. Morning tours best. Also see the Candelabra geoglyph. 'Poor Man's Galápagos'.

sports_and_activities.name: Surfing
best_months: Year-round — northern coast best Oct-Mar, southern coast best Apr-Oct
sports_and_activities.notes: Peru has excellent surf year-round. Northern coast warm (no wetsuit), southern coast cold (full wetsuit). Puerto Chicama has world's longest left.

sports_and_activities.name: Mountaineering
sports_and_activities.notes: Huascarán (6,768m) is Peru's highest. Chachani (6,057m) is a non-technical 6,000er. Acclimatization essential. Hire licensed guides.

sports_and_activities.name: Trekking
sports_and_activities.notes: Peru is one of the world's top trekking destinations. Inca Trail requires advance booking. Huayhuash Circuit is world-class. Altitude is the main challenge.

sports_and_activities.name: Sandboarding
sports_and_activities.notes: Huacachina oasis has massive sand dunes. Combine with dune buggy tours. $20-30 for buggy + sandboarding combo. Best at sunset.

sports_and_activities.name: Paragliding
best_months: Year-round in Lima; May-September in Cusco
sports_and_activities.notes: Lima's Miraflores cliffs are iconic — tandem flights over the ocean and city ($70-90). Stunning at sunset.

sports_and_activities.name: Whitewater Rafting
sports_and_activities.notes: Urubamba is Class II-III (Sacred Valley day trip). Apurímac is Class IV-V (multi-day). Cotahuasi is world-class Class V.

sports_and_activities.name: Birdwatching
sports_and_activities.notes: Peru has 1,800+ bird species — third highest in the world. Manu alone has 1,000+ species. Tambopata macaw clay lick is iconic.

sample_itineraries.one_week: Lima (2 days) → Cusco (2 days acclimatize) → Sacred Valley (1 day) → Machu Picchu (1 day) → Lima (1 day)
sample_itineraries.two_week: Lima (2 days) → Cusco (2 days) → Sacred Valley + Machu Picchu (3 days) → Puno/Lake Titicaca (2 days) → Arequipa (2 days) → Colca Canyon (2 days) → Lima (1 day)
sample_itineraries.classic_route: Lima → Cusco → Sacred Valley → Machu Picchu → Lake Titicaca → Arequipa
sample_itineraries.offbeat_route: Lima → Huaraz (Santa Cruz Trek) → Chachapoyas (Kuélap, Gocta) → Tarapoto → Iquitos (Amazon)

practical_info.arrival_tips: Lima airport (LIM) has authorized taxi counters in arrivals — use Green Taxi or Airport Express bus to Miraflores. Do not accept rides from strangers. ATMs in arrivals hall.
practical_info.airport_scam_notes: Unlicensed taxi drivers approach aggressively in arrivals. Only use official airport taxi counter or pre-booked transfers. Express kidnapping risk with unlicensed taxis.
practical_info.local_sim_where_to_buy: Claro, Movistar, or Entel SIM cards at airport kiosks or city shops. Bring passport. $5-10 for data plan. Claro has best coverage.
practical_info.money_exchange_notes: Exchange at banks or licensed casas de cambio. Street money changers exist but risk counterfeit bills. ATMs give best rate — use BCP or BBVA. Carry small bills.

language_helpful_phrases.mate_de_coca_please: Un mate de coca, por favor

laws.drug_laws_strictness: Very strict — possession of any amount of cocaine or marijuana above small personal use threshold carries severe prison sentences. Peru is a transit country — do not accept packages from strangers.
laws.alcohol_laws: Legal age 18. No open container enforcement generally but public drunkenness can cause issues. Ley seca during elections.
laws.drone_rules: Registration required. Prohibited over archaeological sites (Machu Picchu, Nazca, etc.) and in national parks without special permit. Altitude limit 120m.
laws.wild_camping_rules: Generally permitted in rural areas. Not allowed in archaeological sites or national parks without designated camping areas. Community fees on popular trekking routes.
laws.protected_area_rules: National park/reserve entry fees apply. Inca Trail strictly regulated — permits required. Manu requires licensed operator. Stay on marked trails at archaeological sites.
photography_restricted_sites: Some archaeological sites charge photo fees
laws.lgbtq_legal_status: No legal recognition of same-sex relationships. No anti-discrimination protections. Varying social acceptance — Lima most tolerant.
laws.marijuana_legality: Small amounts (up to 8g) for personal use not prosecuted. Sale, cultivation, and transport illegal. Do not carry.
laws.prescription_medication_rules: Bring prescriptions in original packaging with doctor's letter. Controlled substances require additional documentation. Coca leaf products cannot be exported.

family_travel.car_seat_rules: Required for children but not consistently enforced. Bring your own for car rentals.
family_activities: Ballestas Islands boat tour — sea lions, penguins, seabirds
Sacred Valley — Ollantaytambo ruins, weaving demonstrations, corn mazes
Huacachina — dune buggy rides and sandboarding
Lake Titicaca — visiting Uros floating islands

accessibility.wheelchair_accessibility: Very limited — cobblestone streets in Cusco, uneven sidewalks, steep terrain. Lima (Miraflores) has some accessible infrastructure. Archaeological sites mostly inaccessible.
accessibility.accessible_transport: Lima Metropolitano BRT has some accessibility. Most intercity buses are not wheelchair accessible. Private transfers recommended.
accessible_attractions: Ballestas Islands boat tours (varies by boat)
